coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots-MoS2 nanoflowers as a fluorescence sensor with an off/on switch for intracellular glutathione detection and fabrication of molecular logic gates
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Shipeng
Yu, Chunlei
Qian, Liangliang
Zhou, Chaoqun
Zhen, Zhengan
Liu, Bin
Cheng, Xiumei
Cheng, Rumei
coredata.dc:description: We designed and evaluated a high-performing and biocompatible sensing platform to quantitatively detect glutathione (GSH) in the intracellular environment. To this end, we constructed a fluorescent switch using nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as fluorescent probes and MoS2 nanoflowers as the GSH recognition unit. Fluorescence emission of N-GQDs was first switched OFF when combined with MoS2 nanoflowers due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. It was readily switched ON by reducing MoS2 to Mo3+ in the presence of GSH, enabling the recovery of fluorescent signal. We performed experiments to test the dependence of the sensing platform fluorescence intensity on the concentrations of GSH in the range of 0.4–4.4 mM. The detection limit was 2.47 μM. The monitoring of concentrations of GSH in human retinal pigment epithelium cells (ARPE-19) samples revealed excellent sensing performance with facilitated visualization of the probe, suggesting that the unique sensing strategy has the exceptional anti-interference property for assessing the intracellular GSH quantity in diagnostics and biomedical research. The OFF/ON switch for the detection of GSH was explored to create a XNOR molecular logic gate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-Doped Graphene Quantum Dots
Sensor
Glutathione
Logic Gate
Intracellular

The authors are very grateful for Dr. long Zhang for fruitful discussion.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) AFM images of N-GQDs; (c) the height of N-GQDs; (d) SEM and (e) TEM image of MoS2 nanoflowers; (f) TEM of N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposite.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of (i) N-GQDs, (ii) MoS2, and (iii) N-GQDs-MoS2; (b) FL emission spectra of (i) N-GQDs, (ii) N-GQDs-MoS2, and (iii) N-GQDs-MoS2-GSH. The inset of (b) shows corresponding color of samples photographs under a 365 nm UV lamp; (c) fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs with different concentration MoS2; (d) linear relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency and the concentrations of MoS2; (e) FL emission spectra of N-GQDs-MoS2 upon addition of different concentrations of GSH; (f) relative FL intensity with respect to different GSH concentrations ((
F -
F 0
) /
F 0
= 0.165 ×
C
GSH
+ 0.1
, R2 = 0.99). （The concentrations of N-GQDs is 20 mg/L, λex = 334 nm, λem = 438 nm).

(a) Selectivity of GSH and (b) relative fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs-MoS2 sensing platform in presence of the interfering substances (1; GSH; 2; Glu; 3; Gly; 4; His; 5; Ile; 6; Leu; 7; Met; 8; Cys; 9; Pro; 10; Asp; 11; Thr; 12; Ala; 13; Val; 14; Arg; 15; glucose; 16; maltose; 17; sucrose; 18; galactose; 19; AsA; 20; CA. Red column: sensor + interferent; green column: sensor + interferent + GSH). (The concentration of N-GQDs, MoS2 nanoflower, and GSH are 20 mg/L, 2.5 mg/L, and 4.0 mM, respectively; and the concentration of interferent is 8.0 mM. λex = 334 nm, λem = 438 nm.)

Confocal fluorescence microscopy images of ARPE-19 cells incubated with (i) N-GQDs, (ii) N-GQDs for 12 h, followed by MoS2 for 4 h, (iii) N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposites for 12 h, followed by GSH for 30 min. (Scale: 10 μm).
Schematic illustration of nanocomposite N-GQD-MoS2 and recognition mechanism for GSH detection.
Comparison of different fluorescence sensors for GSH detection.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots-MoS2 nanoflowers as a fluorescence sensor with an off/on switch for intracellular glutathione detection and fabrication of molecular logic gates

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Materials for Nano-Bio Applications, School of Ophthalmology & Optometry, State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Visual Science, Wenzhou Medical University, 270 Xueyuan Xi Road, Wenzhou, Zhejiang 325027, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Materials for Nano-Bio Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Advanced Materials for Nano-Bio Applications, School of Ophthalmology & Optometry, State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Visual Science, Wenzhou Medical University, 270 Xueyuan Xi Road, Wenzhou, Zhejiang 325027, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ruiran (Shanghai) Environmental Engineering Technology Co., Ltd., Lane 88, China (Shanghai) Pilot Free Trade Zone, Shanghai 200120, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ruiran (Shanghai) Environmental Engineering Technology Co., Ltd.
Lane 88, China (Shanghai) Pilot Free Trade Zone

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ruiran (Shanghai) Environmental Engineering Technology Co., Ltd., Lane 88, China (Shanghai) Pilot Free Trade Zone, Shanghai 200120, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Shipeng Tang and Chunlei Yu contribute equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We designed and evaluated a high-performing and biocompatible sensing platform to quantitatively detect glutathione (GSH) in the intracellular environment. To this end, we constructed a fluorescent switch using nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as fluorescent probes and MoS2 nanoflowers as the GSH recognition unit. Fluorescence emission of N-GQDs was first switched OFF when combined with MoS2 nanoflowers due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. It was readily switched ON by reducing MoS2 to Mo3+ in the presence of GSH, enabling the recovery of fluorescent signal. We performed experiments to test the dependence of the sensing platform fluorescence intensity on the concentrations of GSH in the range of 0.4–4.4 mM. The detection limit was 2.47 μM. The monitoring of concentrations of GSH in human retinal pigment epithelium cells (ARPE-19) samples revealed excellent sensing performance with facilitated visualization of the probe, suggesting that the unique sensing strategy has the exceptional anti-interference property for assessing the intracellular GSH quantity in diagnostics and biomedical research. The OFF/ON switch for the detection of GSH was explored to create a XNOR molecular logic gate.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH), an abundant cellular thiol tripeptide, plays a crucial role in pathological and biological processes [1]. For instance, it serves and assists in important cellular functions in the defense against free radicals and oxidative stresses, and toxins. GSH blood and plasma levels in healthy individuals lie within of 684–2525 and 2.22–11.36 μM, respectively [2,3]. GSH levels in living organisms that are outside of these bounds lead to a series of diseases such as Alzheimer's disease, heart problems, cancer, diabetes, etc [4,5]. Most importantly, the overexpression of GSH in organisms is used for the diagnosis of cancer. Thus, the highly sensitive and rapid detection of GSH is in highly demand and of great importance. Currently, a number of detection techniques including high performance liquid chromatography [6], capillary electrophoresis [7], electrochemistry [4], and mass spectrometry [8] have been used for the detection of GSH content in various biological samples. However, most of these methods come at high cost, are complicated, require professional operators, and difficult for the intracellular detection of GSH, which limits their practical applications in the field of life sciences.

Fluorescence assays offer high sensitivity, good selectivity, fast response, and non-invasiveness, and they have developed into one of the most potential analytical methods for the detection of various biomolecules containing sulfhydryl groups. Recently, Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as a novel class fluorescent nanomaterials exhibited extraordinary optical and electrical characteristics, and high conductivity. Compared to toxic transition metal quantum dots, N-GQDs show good biocompatibility, low toxicity, surface functionalities, and are low-cost, which offers promising applications in various fields, such as sensing, bioimaging, catalysis, drug delivery, and optoelectronic devices [9–15].

The combination of fluorescent probes and nanomaterials significantly improves the sensitivity and selectivity of the former for the detection of the analyte [16]. The transition metal dichalcogenides MS2 (M = V, W, Ti, and Mo) have attracted significant attention in recent years owing to their good conductivity, excellent chemical and thermal stability, and catalytic performance. Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), a typical transition metal disulfide with good electron conductivity and high surface area, has widespread uses in the construction of nanocomposites based on van der Waals interactions [17]. It also has promising applications in the development of a switch for the fluorescent sensing platform [18,19]. For instance, Wu et al prepared MoS2/rGO nanocomposite as the photocatalyst to produce ⋅OH radicals for the detection GSH with reducing ability based on the redox reaction [20]. Zhu group and Tang group used MoS2 quantum dots and MnO2 nanosheets to construct MoS2/MnO2 nanosensor for GSH detection based on the inner filter effect (IFE) and redox reaction mechanism [21,22]. However, these reports hardly mentioned the cytotoxicity evaluation of nanomaterials and the detection of intracellular GSH.

In this study, we constructed a simple and selective sensing platform using N-GQDs and MoS2 nanoflowers with an off/on switch for the rapid and sensitive intracellular detection of GSH. The interaction mechanism of nanocomposite N-GQDs-MoS2 as a sensing platform for the detection of GSH is shown in Scheme 1 . The MoS2 nanoflowers was prepared by a one-step hydrothermal technique. MoS2 nanoflowers performs as a nanoquencher and GSH recognition unit. N-GQDs showed good adsorption capacity on the MoS2 surface based on van der Waals interactions. Consequently, the fluorescence of N-GQDs is evidently quenched upon addition of MoS2 nanoflowers into the solution. The affinity between S and H is higher than that between Mo and H [23]. GSH can reduce MoS2 to Mo3+ and release N-GQDs, accompanying the fluorescence recovery of N-GQDs. The fluorescence quenching by MoS2 nanoflowers and recovering by GSH exhibit the potential application of the current system as a logic-driven sensor. As an initial proof of concept, the performance of the nanocomposite N-GQDs-MoS2 sensing platform is suitability for logic operations by assessing GSH. The developed nanocomposite sensing platform shows good biocompatibility, offering a promising application for GSH early monitoring and GSH-related disease clinical diagnostics.

Thiourea and sodium molybdate (VI) dihydrate were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). GSH, trisodium citrate dihydrate, glucose (Glu), glycine (Gly), histidine (His), isoleucine (Ile), leucine (Leu), methionine (Met), cysteine (Cys), proline (Pro), aspartic acid (Asp), threonine (Thr), alanine (Ala), valine (Val), arginine (Arg), maltose, sucrose, galactose, ascorbic acid(AsA) and citric acid (CA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. The other reagents are analytically pure. All solutions were freshly prepared before use.

The fluorescence emission spectra were acquired using a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The UV–vis adsorption spectra were recorded on an Agilent Cary 100 spectrometer. The morphology of N-GQDs and MoS2 were measured utilizing a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100). Surface morphology measurement of MoS2 nanoflowers were performed with a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (NANO SEM430, FEI). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded by the Perkin-Elmer Lambda 35 spectrometer in the range 400–4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALB MK-II spectrometer. X-ray diffraction analysis was performed with a Rigaku D/max 2550 V instrument with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å).

MoS2 nanoflowers were prepared according to the previously published method [24]. Na2MoO4⋅2H2O (0.6 g), (NH2)2CS (0.75 g), NH2OH⋅HCl (0.725 g) as reaction precursors, and distilled water (30 mL) as reaction solvent were used to fabricate MoS2 nanoflowers. First, reaction precursors were dissolved into the solvent, accompanied with magnetic stirring for 30 min. The temperature was maintained at 200 °C for 18 h after the mixture solution was transferred into a 50 mL PTFE-lined autoclave. MoS2 black precipitates were filtered, followed by three washes using distilled water and ethanol.

The N-GQDs was prepared by a typical hydrothermal method [25]. Briefly, 0.21 g (1 mmol) of citric acid and 0.18 g (3 mmol) of urea were dissolved into 10 mL of Nanopure water. Then, the mixture solution was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept for 4 h at 160 °C in a furnace. Upon naturally cooling to room temperature, purified N-GQDs were obtained through the dialysis for three days.

The N-GQDs stock (15 μL) solution was diluted to 3 mL using distilled water. Then, the different concentrations of MoS2 nanoflowers were added into the above-mentioned solution to evaluate the quenching mechanism. Notably, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs was restored upon addition of different quantities of GSH.

The cells were cultured according to our previous report [26,27]. First, human retinal pigment epithelium cells (ARPE-19) were cultured in 96 well plates for 24 h. Second, different concentrations of the N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposite (25, 50, 75, and 100 µg/mL) were added into the culture medium and incubated for another 24, 48, and 72 h. Subsequently, all cells were cultured in fresh medium containing CCK8 in excess and incubated for 4 h. The optical density of the N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposite was measured after replacement of the dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) solution with medium.

ARPE-19 cells with three groups were incubated in 24-well plates for 24 h. The first two groups cells treated with N-GQDs (10 mg/L) or N-GQDs-MoS2 (10 mg/L) nanocomposites was added into the wells and underwent incubation for 12 h, respectively. For the third group cells, firstly, cells incubated with N-GQDs for 12 h and were washed three times with PBS, after which MoS2 (30 mg/L, 10 μL) and GSH (0.25 M, 50 μL) were added consecutively into the culture solution for excess incubating 4 h. The cell images were recorded through confocal laser scanning microscopy.

The morphology of N-GQDs was assessed by TEM and AFM (Fig. 1 ). TEM results showed that the N-GQDs were spherical and monodispersed. The AFM image shows the height profile of N-GQDs at ~ 3–4 nm, suggesting that their structure consists of multilayer graphene (Fig. 1b and1c). The morphologies of MoS2 are depicted in Fig. 1d and 1e, the latter showing a flower-like shape with a diameter of approximately 1.0 μm. Fig. 1f shows the TEM image confirming the formation of the N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposite. As can be seen from the Fig.S1a, the XRD results show that the diffraction peaks of MoS2 nanoflowers at about13.47°, 36.06°, 43.11° and 58.32° were found, ascribing to the (002), (100), (103) and (110) plants of MoS2, respectively [24]. As for the N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposite, XRD results show additional peak at approximately 23.28°, which is assigned to the diffraction peak of N-GQDs, indicating the successful formation of the N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposite. In Fig. S1b, the IR spectra of the N-GQDs exhibits an obviously characteristic peak at 1640 cm−1 ascribed to the C = O stretching vibration. However, it broadened upon addition of MoS2 nanoflowers, indicating the interaction between the N-GQDs and MoS2 nanoflowers. The peak at 1640 cm−1 recovers with the addition of GSH in the N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposite, suggesting the release of N-GQDs from the surface of MoS2 nanoflowers.

To further understand the surface electric states and element composition, we performed XPS characterization. The XPS spectrum of N-GQDs show main peaks ascribed to carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. Compared to the N-GQDs, the XPS spectrum of the N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposite exhibits additional peaks corresponding to molybdenum and sulfur elements (Fig. S2a). In N-GQDs, C1s peaks can be fitted into five different components corresponding to C-O (284.32 eV), C-OH (284.62 eV), C-N (285.92 eV), C = O (287.82 eV), and C(O)O (288.7 eV), as shown in Fig. S2b. The high-resolution N1s spectrum was fitted into two peaks, ascribed to the pyrrolic N (399.72 eV) and graphitic N (401.72 eV) (Fig. S2c). Fig. S2d shows the peaks at 227.27, 229.97, and 233.32 eV ascribed to S2p, Mo 3d5/2, and Mo 3d3/2, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of GSH and effect of pH
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 2 a shows UV–vis absorption peaks of N-GQDs at 337 and 233 nm, ascribed to the π-π transition of aromatic sp2 carbons and the n-π transition of pyrrolic N, respectively. MoS2 nanoflowers showed characteristic absorption peaks at 213 nm. The fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQDs, N-GQDs-MoS2, and N-GQDs-MoS2 + GSH are shown in Fig. 2b. The free N-GQDs exhibit maximum fluorescence emission peaks at 439 nm, and the fluorescence of N-GQDs is evidently quenched upon addition of MoS2. The fluorescence recovery of N-GQDs is observed after the addition of GSH. This phenomenon is explained in terms of the redox reaction between GSH and MoS2. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs decreases with the increasing concentration of MoS2 from 0.23 mg/L to 1.28 mg/L (Fig. 2c). The linear relationship is given by
( F
0
- F ) /
F 0
= 0.248 ×
C
M o S 2
+ 0.282
with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.99 (Fig. 2d). F0 and F denote the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of MoS2 nanoflowers, respectively. To further examine the fluorescence quenching behavior of N-GQDs in the presence of MoS2, the fluorescence lifetime characterization of N-GQDs were performed with and without of MoS2 (Fig. S3). The two decay time values are very close, which could be attributed to nonradiative energy or electron transfer process.

To optimize the detection of GSH, the effect of pH on the relative fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs-MoS2 in the presence of GSH was investigated. Fig. S4 shows the relative fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs-MoS2, which was clearly enhanced between pH 5.0 to 6.5 in the presence of GSH. The relative fluorescence intensity gradually decreased within the pH range from 6.5 to 8.0. These results may be attributed to the different reducibilities of the sulfhydryl group of GSH at different pH values. pH 6.5 was selected as the optimal pH value for the detection of GSH.

To confirm the application of this designed fluorescence sensing platform for the detection of GSH, we studied the fluorescence response behavior of the N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposite at different concentrations of GSH. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs was gradually recovered with the increase of GSH concentration (Fig. 2e). The recovery of the fluorescence intensity depends on the increasing concentration of the GSH within 0.4 mM to 4.4 mM. The plot of relative FL intensity with respect to different concentrations of GSH is shown in Fig. 2f. The linear relationship equation was
( F -
F 0
)
/
F 0
= 0.165 ×
C
GSH
+ 0.1
, R2 = 0.99. F and F0 denote the fluorescence intensities with and without of GSH, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was 2.47 μM, given by the equation LOD = 3σ/s. As a contrast, we studied the fluorescence response behavior of N-GQDs at different GSH concentrations. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs decreased slightly upon addition of GSH (Fig. S5).

Moreover, the effectiveness of the proposed sensing platform was confirmed by comparing different sensors, listed in Table 1 , with several current results. Compared to the literature, our study yielded a generally linear range and detection limit. Furthermore, our method showed excellent detection performance for intracellular GSH. Thus, it would be good to “outperform” other methods for the detection of GSH in living cells.

We further evaluated the selectivity of the N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposite as a fluorescence sensing platform for the detection of GSH. Selectivity experiments were performed on various biomolecules including GSH, Glu, Gly, His, Ile, Leu, Met, Cys, Pro, Asp, Thr, Ala, Val, Arg, glucose, maltose, sucrose, galactose, AsA and CA. Experimental results show that N-GQDs-MoS2 exhibits a high fluorescence response in response to GSH. Meanwhile, no evident fluorescence response was observed with other molecules (Fig. 3 a). Furthermore, competitive experiments were carried out in the mixture solution. N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposites maintained their high selectivity for GSH, even in environments with significant interference from other molecules, suggesting that the proposed sensing platform can be applied as a highly selective fluorescence sensor for the detection of GSH without interference from other analytes (Fig. 3b).

We have constructed the XNOR logic gate with GSH and MoS2 as two inputs for the sensing platform (Fig. S6a). The fluorescence intensity of 100 was defined as the threshold. For the outputs, the fluorescence intensity at 438 nm emission below and above the threshold is defined as “0″ (off state) and “1” (on state), respectively. Thus, (0, 0), (0, 1), (1, 0), and (1, 1) are four possible input combinations shown in Fig. S6b. In the absence of any input (0, 0), fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs as output, FL at 438 nm shows an output of 1. Upon addition of MoS2 (0, 1), the FL at 438 nm is lower than the threshold, generating the output of 0. Moreover, when the input is (1, 0), the fluorescence intensity at 438 nm remains higher than the threshold value, and the output was kept as 1. In the presence of GSH and MoS2 inputs (1, 1), the fluorescence intensity at 438 nm is higher than the threshold, and output was changed to “1”. All these results confirm that N-GQDs-MoS2 forms the OFF/ON switch and can be used to construct the XNOR logic gate function. The corresponding truth table is shown in Fig. S6c.

The cytotoxicity of N-GQDs and N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposites was investigated using the CCK8 assay. Fig. S7a and S7b show the ARPE-19 cells incubated with different concentrations N-GQDs and N-GQDs-MoS2, respectively, for different durations. The viability results of N-GQDs or the N-GQDs-MoS2 treated cells show no apparent loss, even incubated 72 h at the concentration of 100 μg/mL. Hence, N-GQDs and N-GQDs-MoS2 show good biocompatibility and low toxicity and can thus be used in intracellular detection of GSH.

The capability of N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposites to serve as a fluorescence sensing platform to detect intracellular GSH has been further confirmed in vitro. ARPE-19 cells were dyed red using rhodamine phalloidin, as shown in Fig. 4 a, d, g and Fig. S8a, d, g. The overlayed images are shown in Fig. 4c, f, i and Fig. S8c, f, i. First, ARPE-19 cells were incubated with N-GQDs for 12 h in an incubator. The cells exhibited a bright blue fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 4b. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs obviously decreased when cells were incubated with MoS2 for another 4 h (Fig. 4e). However, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs recovered once GSH was added into the culture medium of N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposites (Fig. 4h).

ARPE-19 cells only incubated with N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposites served as a control group. Fig. S8b shows that weak fluorescence signal was observed for ARPE-19 cells incubated only with N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposites. However, a significant fluorescence signal of ARPE-19 cells treated with N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposites was obtained upon the addition of exogenous GSH into these cells. Furthermore, a significant fluorescence intensity enhancement for ARPE-19 cells incubated with N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposites appeared when different concentrations of GSH were added into the culture medium (Fig. S8e and S8h). The confocal microscopy images showed no obvious cell morphology changes. These results implied that N-GQDS and N-GQDs-MoS2 have low toxicity and good biocompatibility, and can be used to detect intracellular GSH in living cells.

We constructed a rapid and convenient sensing platform for the detection of GSH in living cells based on the self-assembly N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposites. N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposites as a sensing platform detect the intracellular GSH redox reaction. MoS2 nanoflowers were synthesized by the hydrothermal method and characterized by SEM and TEM analysis. N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposites as a sensing platform show high selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of GSH. The LOD is 2.47 μM. The fluorescence of N-GQDs was considerably quenched and enhanced by the sequential presence of MoS2 and GSH, due to the FRET and redox reaction, respectively. Furthermore, the N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposites sensing platform can be constructed as a XNOR logic gate using MoS2 and GSH as inputs and the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs as outputs, indicating that the N-GQDs-MoS2 nanocomposites sensing platform has potential applications as logic devices in medical diagnosis and biomedical research.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405115), the Wenzhou Bureau of Science and Technology (Y20210081), the technology project of Zhejiang Province Medical and Health Project (2015KYB254, 2017KY492), project of State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Vision Science of Wenzhou Medical University (J02-20210202) and Ophthalmology & Optometry of Wenzhou Medical University (YNCX201408) are acknowledged. The authors are very grateful for Dr. long Zhang for fruitful discussion.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106786.
